title: Lesson 1 Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Welcome, Luis. This is your first Maltese lesson — a guided walkthrough of what you'll be learning. Take your time, you can pause whenever you need.
We'll cover four things today: how to introduce yourself, the Maltese alphabet, the word for 'the', and the days of the week.
First, introductions. To say 'hello', you say
for 'good morning'. To tell someone your name, you say
which means 'my name is Luis'. To ask someone their name:
Now, the Maltese alphabet. It has thirty letters — twenty-four consonants and six vowels: a, e, i, o, u, and the special vowel 'ie' which counts as one even though it's two symbols.
Five letters are unique to Maltese. The first is
the c with a dot, like in
the g with a dot, soft like the j in 'judge', as in
the h with a stroke — pronounced like a strong English h, as in
the z with a dot, voiced like the z in zoo, as in
which counts as one consonant. It often lengthens the vowel next to it.
Most Maltese words are pronounced just as they're written, which makes spelling easier than English. The language has Semitic roots from Arabic, mixed with Italian and English loan-words.
Next, the article — the word for 'the'. Maltese has three forms, depending on the first letter of the next word.
Rule one: before moon consonants — that's b, f, g, ġ, ħ, j, k, l, m, p, q, v, and w — you use
Rule two: before a vowel, before the letter h, or before għ, you use
Rule three: before sun consonants — that's ċ, d, n, r, s, t, x, ż, and z — the article assimilates, meaning the L of il- becomes the same letter as the noun. So
Don't worry about memorising all of this at once — the exercises will drill it into you naturally.
Finally, the days of the week. Listen and repeat each one as you hear it.
Notice how the article changes: It-, L-, Il-, Is- — all the rules you just learned. Now jump into the section that interests you most. You've got this. Saħħa.
